name: Linked List Basics — Node, Traversal, Dummy Head

metadata.career_relevance: Linked list questions appear in every Flipkart and Amazon India DSA round. The dummy head pattern eliminates edge cases in insert/delete — knowing it signals experience. Java's LinkedList uses a doubly-linked list internally; understanding the node structure helps you know when to use it vs ArrayList.

hook.real_world_problem:
```
Zerodha order book: insert/delete orders at arbitrary positions
ArrayList: O(n) insert at position — shifts all elements
LinkedList: O(1) insert given a pointer — just rewire pointers

Dummy head pattern:
  Without dummy: special-case for empty list and head deletion
  With dummy:    head = dummy.next; delete = prev.next = curr.next
  Result: same code handles ALL cases — no if(head==null)
```
hook.what_you_will_build: A linked list from scratch with insert, delete, reverse, and all classic operations.

visual_aid.description: Singly linked list 1→2→3→null. Node structure: [val|next]. Dummy node pattern: [dummy|→] → [1|→] → [2|→] → [3|null]. Delete node 2: prev.next = curr.next. Insert after 1: newNode.next=curr.next; curr.next=newNode.
visual_aid.alt_text: Linked list with dummy head pattern

worked_example.scenario: Delete N-th node from end in one pass.
worked_example.problem_statement: ListNode head, int n → remove nth node from end.

step_by_step_solution.details: fast pointer advances n steps. Then advance both until fast reaches end. slow.next is the target.

things_to_notice: Dummy head: slow.next=slow.next.next works even when deleting the real head
Gap n+1 between fast and slow: fast is at null when slow.next is target
One pass: O(n) time, O(1) space

industry_examples.use_case: Order cancellation at arbitrary position: O(1) with pointer vs O(n) array shift.

applications.interview_relevance: Every LL interview starts with 'implement from scratch.' Dummy head pattern is the key insight that separates prepared candidates.
applications.real_world_workflow: LL checklist: (1) Always use dummy head, (2) track prev for deletion, (3) check null before .next access, (4) return dummy.next not head.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Accessing curr.next without checking curr!=null first. NPE crashes linked list solutions in interviews.
common_gaps.remediation: Pattern: while(curr!=null) NOT while(curr.next!=null) unless you need the last non-null node. Always add null checks before .next access.

key_takeaways: ListNode: val + next. Doubly: val + next + prev
LinkedList O(1) insert/delete given pointer; O(n) access by index
ArrayList O(1) access; O(n) insert/delete in middle

self_check_questions.question: Why does the dummy head pattern simplify linked list code?
self_check_questions.answer: The dummy head ensures prev is never null. Without dummy, deleting the real head requires special-case code: if(curr==head) head=head.next. With dummy, the real head is dummy.next, and every node (including the real head) has a prev node (at minimum, dummy). So the delete operation prev.next=curr.next works uniformly for all nodes — no special cases needed.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Two-Pointer Techniques on Linked Lists — fast/slow runners for cycle detection, middle node, nth from end.
